ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for baked pasta with tomato sauce and mushrooms; closest parent 'pasta food product' (FOODON_00002187) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Pasta, cooked) is the closest defensible parent; no specific code exists for baked pasta with tomato and mushroom toppings.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226754001 'Pasta dish' is the closest parent; no specific code for baked pasta with tomato sauce and mushrooms.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of baked pasta is approximately 350 g, comprising a generous portion of pasta in tomato-mushroom sauce topped with melted cheese, as typically served at a casual dining restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Pasta with tomato sauce, baked, with cheese — composite estimate based on USDA FoodData Central entries: Pasta, cooked (FDC ID 168936), Tomato sauce, canned (FDC ID 170457), Mushrooms, cooked (FDC ID 169251), Mozzarella cheese (FDC ID 171242)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for baked pasta with tomato sauce and mushrooms as a composite dish. Values estimated from a weighted blend of USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked pasta (~55% by weight), tomato sauce (~25%), cooked mushrooms (~10%), and mozzarella cheese (~10%), reflecting a typical restaurant preparation. Energy cross-checked against NCC Food and Nutrient Database category anchors for baked pasta dishes. Sodium elevated to reflect restaurant-level seasoning.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — pasta, cooked (al dente), tomato-based sauce; Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K et al.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for baked pasta with tomato sauce and mushrooms as a composite dish. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked pasta with tomato sauce (GI ~40–50, reference food glucose=100). Baking with cheese slightly increases the GI relative to plain boiled pasta but the tomato-mushroom sauce and cheese fat content moderate the glycemic response. GI 45 selected as the central defensible estimate. GL per serving calculated as: (45 × 19.5 × 350) / (100 × 100) = 30.7 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~380 mg/100 g; ~1330 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a standard serving and request reduced-salt preparation, as a full restaurant serving provides approximately 1330 mg sodium — over half the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~30.7 per serving) and refined carbohydrate content (~68 g per serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a reduced portion (150–200 g) to limit glycemic load, and pair the dish with a non-starchy vegetable side to blunt the postprandial glucose response; the low GI of 45 is favourable but the large carbohydrate mass per restaurant serving elevates total glycemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from cheese (~2.4 g/100 g; ~8.4 g per serving) and elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should be aware that a full serving contributes approximately 8.4 g saturated fat and 1330 mg sodium; choosing a smaller portion and requesting less cheese can meaningfully reduce saturated fat and sodium intake in line with AHA dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Baked Pasta with Tomato Sauce and Mushrooms is a Western-style baked pasta dish served at casual dining restaurants in Singapore, notably at Flavours by Saute at Funan Mall. The dish consists of cooked pasta — typically penne or rigatoni — tossed in a rich tomato-based sauce with sautéed mushrooms, then topped generously with cheese (commonly mozzarella or a blend) and oven-baked until the cheese forms a golden, bubbling crust. The preparation follows a two-stage cooking process: the pasta and sauce are first cooked separately, then assembled and baked, resulting in a dish with a tender interior and a slightly crisp, caramelised cheese topping. The flavour profile is savoury and umami-rich, with the acidity of the tomato sauce balanced by the earthiness of the mushrooms and the creaminess of the melted cheese. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-energy, carbohydrate-dominant meal providing approximately 142 kcal per 100 g, with meaningful protein from the cheese (~6.2 g/100 g) and moderate saturated fat (~2.4 g/100 g). The glycemic index is estimated at 45 (low), though the glycemic load per full restaurant serving (~350 g) is approximately 30.7, which is clinically significant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. Sodium content is elevated at approximately 380 mg/100 g due to restaurant-level seasoning, making portion control important for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is vegetarian and halal-suitable, and contains gluten and dairy allergens.

confidence_reason: No exact match found in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central for this specific composite baked pasta dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted blend of USDA FoodData Central component entries (cooked pasta, tomato sauce, mushrooms, mozzarella cheese). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for pasta with tomato sauce. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component entries used for this composite estimate. All other -1 values have been replaced with estimated numeric values from USDA FoodData Central component data. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, and all B-vitamins are estimated from USDA component data and should be treated as approximate. Sodium is elevated above the USDA cooked-pasta baseline to reflect restaurant-level seasoning typical of Singapore casual dining. The food_name has been normalised from the menu label 'BP3-Baked Pasta Tomato Shrooms' to the canonical English name 'Baked Pasta with Tomato Sauce and Mushrooms'. The cuisine is classified as Global/Western reflecting the Italian-origin dish adapted for the Singapore market. Multilingual names provided for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi as required for Singapore-served dishes under G6. The dish is served at Flavours by Saute - Funan (field survey data, price SGD 18.70). GI category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule (GI=45). GL per serving = (45 × 19.5 × 350) / (100 × 100) = 30.7.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Drafted full record with USDA composite nutrient estimate, GI analogous estimate, multilingual names for Chinese/Malay/Tamil/Hindi, ontology anchors with closest_parent match types, health context for all three required conditions, and LLM description >200 characters.

Iteration 2: Verified aliases_by_language consistency against flat aliases list — confirmed all entries present verbatim. Verified GI=45 uses gi_category='low' per ≤55 threshold rule. Confirmed no zero core macros requiring justification. Confirmed all match_type values are from the allowed set (exact/closest_parent/none).

Iteration 3: Addressed G3 numeric hygiene failure by explicitly documenting the reason for vitamin_d_mcg = -1 in the 'notes' field, using the phrasing 'as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component entries used for this composite estimate' to align with the critical rule example.
